fault_hypothesis: On line 62, the NaN check uses `loss_value == loss_value` as the non-NaN guard, but this condition is placed as the first part of an AND with `loss_value > max_loss`. When loss_value is NaN, `loss_value == loss_value` is False so the whole condition is False and the function returns True instead of False. The NaN check should be inverted: NaN should cause a return of False, not True.

spec.expr: not (loss_value != loss_value) or (loss_value == loss_value and loss_value > max_loss)

constraints.intent: When loss_value is NaN (loss_value != loss_value is True), the if-branch should have been entered to return False, but the condition on line 62 fails to catch NaN.
After the if-branch is skipped (else path), loss_value must be non-NaN and within max_loss; NaN should never reach this path.
Before using loss_value in the guard condition, NaN or non-negative values are the only valid inputs after validation.
If loss_value is NaN, the function must return False.
